title: How do you build and manage RESTful APIs using Spring MVC?

answers.junior: To build a RESTful API in Spring MVC, you use the @RestController annotation on a class, which combines @Controller and @ResponseBody to ensure that return values are automatically serialized into the response body. Methods are mapped to specific HTTP verbs using annotations such as @GetMapping, @PostMapping, @PutMapping, and @DeleteMapping. Spring handles the conversion between Java objects and formats like JSON or XML using HttpMessageConverters, and you can control response status codes by returning a ResponseEntity object.
answers.middle: Building robust APIs involves detailed request handling and data validation. You use @PathVariable for URI segments, @RequestParam for query parameters, and @RequestBody to capture the incoming payload. To ensure data integrity, you should apply JSR-303/JSR-380 Bean Validation annotations like @NotNull or @Size on your DTOs, triggered by the @Valid or @Validated annotation in the controller. For centralized error handling, use @ControllerAdvice along with @ExceptionHandler methods to catch exceptions globally and return consistent error structures with appropriate HTTP status codes, such as 400 for validation errors or 404 for missing resources.
answers.senior: Advanced REST implementation in Spring MVC should follow the Richardson Maturity Model, ideally aiming for Level 3 by using Spring HATEOAS to provide hypermedia links for resource discovery. Senior developers must implement content negotiation to support multiple media types via the Accept header and manage API versioning through strategies like URI versioning, custom headers, or media type versioning. Performance should be optimized by managing Jackson serialization settings and ensuring that controllers, which are singleton beans, remain stateless and thread-safe. Additionally, you should configure Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) globally or at the controller level and automate API documentation using OpenAPI 3.0 via the SpringDoc library to provide an interactive Swagger UI for consumers.
answers.tricky: A common tricky scenario involves the execution order of @ControllerAdvice; if multiple advice classes exist, you must use the @Order annotation or implement the Ordered interface to prevent the wrong handler from intercepting an exception. Another subtle issue is the difference between @PathVariable and @RequestParam regarding URI encoding: path variables are part of the resource identity and follow different encoding rules than query parameters. Developers often fail to distinguish between PUT for full replacements and PATCH for partial updates, which may require using Map-based inputs or JSON Merge Patch to avoid wiping out null fields. Furthermore, circular dependencies in HATEOAS resource models can lead to infinite recursion during JSON serialization. Finally, while Spring manages the request-response cycle, the lack of distributed transactions across multiple REST calls requires implementing patterns like Saga or using idempotency keys (X-Idempotency-Key) to handle retry logic safely for non-idempotent POST operations. It is also critical to understand that @Transactional will not work on private methods within the controller due to Spring's proxy-based AOP, leading to potential data inconsistency.
